title: Desmodium glutinosum
intro: Desmodium glutinosum, also known as stick-tight or beggar's lice, is a perennial legume native to North America that grows as a vine or shrub, producing small, fragrant flowers. The plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and wounds, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. Bioactive constituents of Desmodium glutinosum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal effects. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to relieve pain, reduce inflammation, and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Desmodium glutinosum is a species of flowering plant in the pea family Fabaceae. It is native to the Americas and has been introduced to other parts of the world. Desmodium glutinosum is a perennial herb that grows in a variety of habitats, including wetlands and dry forests. The plant is classified as a member of the subfamily Faboideae, which is one of the largest and most diverse groups of flowering plants. Further classification of Desmodium glutinosum includes it being a part of the genus Desmodium.

It has been used to treat a range of ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Desmodium glutinosum for medicinal purposes.

side_effects: Desmodium glutinosum, also known as stick-tite, is a plant that may cause gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea and diarrhea, when ingested. Its seeds may cause irritation to the skin and mucous membranes, leading to discomfort and inflammation. Ingesting large quantities of the plant can also cause stomach pain and vomiting in some individuals. The plant's toxicity may vary depending on the individual's sensitivity and the amount consumed. More research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Desmodium glutinosum.
